sentences.chinese: 黯曰：「長安令亡罪，獨斬臣黯，民乃肯出馬。
sentences.literal: An said, "The magistrate of Chang'an is without guilt; only execute your servant An, and then the people will be willing to bring out horses.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An said, "The magistrate of Chang'an is innocent; behead me instead, and the citizens will surrender their horses."

sentences.chinese: 且匈奴畔其主而降漢，徐以縣次傳之，何至令天下騷動，罷中國，甘心夷狄之人乎！」
sentences.literal: Moreover the Xiongnu rebelled against their lord and surrendered to Han—slowly convey them relay by relay from county to county; how did it reach the point of throwing all under heaven into turmoil, exhausting the central states, and making people content to serve barbarians!"
sentences.idiomatic: The tribes broke with their chieftain to join us—they could have been moved post by post, county by county. Why convulse the realm, drain the heartland, and treat them like pampered guests of honor?

sentences.chinese: 上默然。
sentences.literal: The sovereign was silent.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said nothing.

sentences.chinese: 後渾邪王至，賈人與市者，坐當死五百餘人。
sentences.literal: Later when the Hunye king arrived, merchants who traded with them—more than five hundred were sentenced to death.
sentences.idiomatic: When Hunye arrived, over five hundred traders who had dealt with him were condemned to die.

sentences.chinese: 黯入，請間，見高門，曰：「夫匈奴攻當路塞，絕和親，中國舉兵誅之，死傷不可勝計，而費以巨萬百數。
sentences.literal: An entered, requested a private audience, saw him at Gaomen, and said, "When the Xiongnu attacked the strategic passes and severed peace and kinship, the central states raised troops to punish them—dead and wounded beyond counting, and expense reckoned in hundreds of millions.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An forced a private audience at Gaomen and said, "When the nomads seized the frontier passes and broke the marriage treaties, Han raised armies until the butcher's bill and the treasury toll ran beyond reckoning."

sentences.chinese: 臣愚以為陛下得胡人，皆以為奴婢，賜從軍死者家；
sentences.literal: In my foolish view I thought that when Your Majesty obtained Hu people you would make them all slaves and bondmaids and bestow them on the families of those who died in the army;
sentences.idiomatic: I assumed you would enslave the captives and hand them to the kin of the fallen,

sentences.chinese: 鹵獲，因與之，以謝天下，塞百姓之心。
sentences.literal: and battle booty—thereby give it to them—to give satisfaction to all under heaven and assuage the common people.
sentences.idiomatic: or distribute the spoils to the same end—to balance the scales for the empire and quiet public resentment.

sentences.chinese: 今縱不能，渾邪帥數萬之眾來，虛府庫賞賜，發良民侍養，若奉驕子。
sentences.literal: Now even if you cannot do that, Hunye leading tens of thousands to come—you empty the treasuries in rewards, dispatch good commoners to wait on and nurture them, as if serving a spoiled child.
sentences.idiomatic: Instead you drain the vaults to reward them, press good farmers into service as their valets, and coddle them like spoiled heirs.

sentences.chinese: 愚民安知市買長安中而文吏繩以為闌出財物如邊關乎？
sentences.literal: How should ignorant commoners know that buying in Chang'an would have clerkly officials bind them as illegally exporting goods like at the frontier passes?
sentences.idiomatic: How was a shopkeeper in Chang'an to guess that a street purchase would be twisted into 'smuggling goods past the border,' the way clerks treat frontier trade?

sentences.chinese: 陛下縱不能得匈奴之贏以謝天下，又以微文殺無知者五百餘人，臣竊為陛下弗取也。」
sentences.literal: Though Your Majesty could not take Xiongnu spoils to give satisfaction to all under heaven, you further use fine-print statutes to kill more than five hundred who did not know—privately I deem this unworthy of Your Majesty."
sentences.idiomatic: You gain no Xiongnu plunder to appease the people, yet you kill five hundred innocents on legal technicalities—I cannot call that worthy of you.

sentences.chinese: 上弗許，曰：「吾久不聞汲黯之言，今又復妄發矣。」
sentences.literal: The sovereign did not grant it, saying, "I have long not heard Ji An's words; now he has again shot off rashly."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor waved it off: "I have missed Ji An's voice—but there he goes again, sounding off without restraint."

sentences.chinese: 後數月，黯坐小法，會赦，免官。
sentences.literal: Several months later An was charged with a minor legal offense; there happened to be an amnesty; he was dismissed from office.
sentences.idiomatic: Months later Ji An caught a petty legal charge; a general amnesty spared him, but he lost his post.

sentences.chinese: 於是黯隱於田園者數年。
sentences.literal: Thereupon An hid in fields and gardens for several years.
sentences.idiomatic: He withdrew to his estate for several years.

sentences.chinese: 會更立五銖錢，民多盜鑄錢者，楚地尤甚。
sentences.literal: There happened to be a change establishing the five-zhu coin; the people often counterfeited coins; Chu territory was especially severe.
sentences.idiomatic: When the new five-zhu coin was issued, illicit minting spread, worst of all through Chu.

sentences.chinese: 上以為淮陽，楚地之郊也，召黯拜為淮陽太守。
sentences.literal: The sovereign thought Huaiyang lay on the marches of Chu territory, summoned An, and appointed him governor of Huaiyang.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor judged Huaiyang the gateway into Chu and summoned Ji An to serve as its governor.

sentences.chinese: 黯伏謝不受印綬，詔數強予，然後奉詔。
sentences.literal: An knelt and declined, refusing to accept the seal-ribbon; the edict repeatedly forced it on him; then he obeyed the edict.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An kowtowed and tried to refuse the seal until repeated edicts left him no choice but to accept.

sentences.chinese: 召上殿，黯泣曰：「臣自以為填溝壑，不復見陛下，不意陛下復收之。
sentences.literal: Summoned to ascend the hall, An wept and said, "I had thought I would fill ditches and gullies and never again see Your Majesty; I did not expect Your Majesty would take me back.
sentences.idiomatic: Called before the throne in tears, he said, "I was sure I would die in obscurity and never look on your face again—I never dreamed you would call me back."

sentences.chinese: 臣常有狗馬之心，今病，力不能任郡事。
sentences.literal: I always had the heart of a dog or horse; now I am ill and lack strength to bear commandery affairs.
sentences.idiomatic: My loyalty is still that of your old hound, but I am sick and no longer fit to run a commandery.

sentences.chinese: 臣願為中郎，出入禁闥，補過拾遺，臣之願也。」
sentences.literal: I beg to serve as gentleman of the palace, entering and leaving the inner gates, mending faults and picking up oversights—that is my wish."
sentences.idiomatic: Let me serve as a palace gentleman instead—passing through the inner gates, catching small slips before they grow—that is all I dare ask.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「君薄淮陽邪？
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, "Do you look down on Huaiyang?"
sentences.idiomatic: "Do you think Huaiyang beneath you?"

sentences.chinese: 吾今召君矣。
sentences.literal: I have now summoned you.
sentences.idiomatic: I have brought you here for a reason.

sentences.chinese: 顧淮陽吏民不相得，吾徒得君重，臥而治之。」
sentences.literal: Only Huaiyang's clerks and people do not get along—I merely need your weight; lie down and govern it."
sentences.idiomatic: The place is torn between its magistrates and the people; I need nothing but the weight of your name—govern it even from your couch if you must.

sentences.chinese: 黯既辭，過大行李息，曰：「黯棄逐居郡，不得與朝廷議矣。
sentences.literal: After An took his leave he passed Grand Herald Li Xi and said, "Cast off to dwell in a commandery, I cannot join court deliberations.
sentences.idiomatic: On his way out Ji An stopped Grand Herald Li Xi: "Banished to the provinces, I shall no longer speak in council."

sentences.chinese: 然御史大夫湯智足以距諫，詐足以飾非，非肯正為天下言，專阿主意。
sentences.literal: Yet Imperial Counsellor Tang's cleverness is sufficient to reject remonstrance, his deceit sufficient to cover faults; he is not willing to speak straight for the empire but solely fawns on the ruler's intent.
sentences.idiomatic: But Zhang Tang is clever enough to block every remonstrance and slick enough to varnish every fault; he will not speak for the realm, only stroke the emperor's mood.

sentences.chinese: 主意所不欲，因而毀之；
sentences.literal: What the ruler's intent dislikes, he thereupon slanders;
sentences.idiomatic: What the throne dislikes, he trashes;

sentences.chinese: 主意所欲，因而譽之。
sentences.literal: what the ruler's intent desires, he thereupon praises.
sentences.idiomatic: what it favors, he praises.

sentences.chinese: 好興事，舞文法，內懷詐以御主心，外挾賊吏以為重。
sentences.literal: He loves to raise affairs and twist the letter of the law; inwardly he harbors deceit to steer the ruler's heart; outwardly he relies on vicious clerks to make his weight.
sentences.idiomatic: He stirs up business, twists statutes, plays on your mind from within, and leans on cruel underlings from without.

sentences.chinese: 公列九卿不早言之何？
sentences.literal: You ranked among the nine ministers—why did you not speak early?
sentences.idiomatic: You sit among the nine ministers—why have you never called him out?

sentences.chinese: 公與之俱受其戮矣！」
sentences.literal: You will with him together face execution!"
sentences.idiomatic: You will share his scaffold if you stay silent!

sentences.chinese: 息畏湯，終不敢言。
sentences.literal: Xi feared Tang and in the end did not dare speak.
sentences.idiomatic: Li Xi feared Zhang Tang too much ever to repeat the warning.

sentences.chinese: 黯居郡如其故治，淮陽政清。
sentences.literal: An dwelt in the commandery governing as before; Huaiyang's government was pure.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An governed Huaiyang in his old style, and the region became a model of honest rule.

sentences.chinese: 後張湯敗，上聞黯與息言，抵息罪。
sentences.literal: Later when Zhang Tang fell, the sovereign heard what An had said to Xi and imposed punishment on Xi.
sentences.idiomatic: When Zhang Tang fell, the emperor remembered Ji An's words to Li Xi and punished Li Xi for his silence.

sentences.chinese: 令黯以諸侯相秩居淮陽。
sentences.literal: He ordered An to dwell in Huaiyang at the salary rank of a chancellor to the feudal lords.
sentences.idiomatic: He let Ji An remain at Huaiyang on the salary scale of a feudal chancellor.

sentences.chinese: 居淮陽十歲而卒。
sentences.literal: He dwelt in Huaiyang ten years and died.
sentences.idiomatic: He died there after ten years.

sentences.chinese: 卒後，上以黯故，官其弟仁至九卿，子偃至諸侯相。
sentences.literal: After his death, because of An the sovereign gave office to his younger brother Ren up to the nine ministers, and his son Yan became chancellor to the feudal lords.
sentences.idiomatic: After his death the emperor honored his memory by promoting his brother Ren among the nine ministers and his son Yan to a princely chancellorship.

sentences.chinese: 黯姊子司馬安亦少與黯為太子洗馬。
sentences.literal: An's elder sister's son Sima An had in youth together with An been groom of the heir apparent.
sentences.idiomatic: His sister's son Sima An had once served with him as groom to the crown prince.

sentences.chinese: 安文深巧善宦，四至九卿，以河南太守卒。
sentences.literal: An was deep in texts, clever, skilled at office; four times he reached the nine ministers, and died as governor of Henan.
sentences.idiomatic: Sima An was a subtle stylist and a born bureaucrat; he rose four times to the nine ministers and died as governor of Henan.

sentences.chinese: 昆弟以安故，同時至二千石十人。
sentences.literal: Cousins because of him at the same time reached two thousand piculs—ten persons.
sentences.idiomatic: On his account as many as ten kinsmen held posts at the two-thousand-picul level at once.

sentences.chinese: 濮陽段宏始事蓋侯信，信任宏，官亦再至九卿。
sentences.literal: Duan Hong of Puyang at first served Marquis of Gai Xin; Xin trusted Hong; Hong's office also twice reached the nine ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: Duan Hong of Puyang began as a follower of Marquis of Gai Xin, won his trust, and himself twice reached the nine ministers.

sentences.chinese: 然衛人仕者皆嚴憚汲黯，出其下。
sentences.literal: Yet among Wei natives who held office all strictly feared Ji An and ranked below him.
sentences.idiomatic: Yet every official from Wei still stood in awe of Ji An and conceded him the first place.

sentences.chinese: 鄭當時
sentences.literal: Zheng Dangshi
sentences.idiomatic: Zheng Dangshi

sentences.chinese: 鄭當時字莊，陳人也。
sentences.literal: Zheng Dangshi, courtesy name Zhuang, was a native of Chen.
sentences.idiomatic: Zheng Dangshi, courtesy name Zhuang, came from Chen commandery.

sentences.chinese: 其先鄭君嘗事項籍，籍死而屬漢。
sentences.literal: His ancestor Lord Zheng had once served Xiang Yu; when Yu died he attached to Han.
sentences.idiomatic: His forebear Lord Zheng had served Xiang Ji; when Ji fell he transferred his allegiance to Han.

sentences.chinese: 高祖令諸故項籍臣名籍，鄭君獨不奉詔。
sentences.literal: Gaozu ordered all former ministers of Xiang Yu to name Yu [by his personal name as contempt]; Lord Zheng alone did not comply with the edict.
sentences.idiomatic: Gaozu ordered Xiang Yu's old retainers to refer to him by his personal name as a sign of submission; Lord Zheng alone refused the edict.

sentences.chinese: 詔盡拜名籍者為大夫，而逐鄭君。
sentences.literal: The edict appointed all who named Yu as grandees and expelled Lord Zheng.
sentences.idiomatic: Those who obeyed were made grandees; Lord Zheng was banished.

sentences.chinese: 鄭君死孝文時。
sentences.literal: Lord Zheng died in Emperor Xiaowen's time.
sentences.idiomatic: Lord Zheng died during the reign of Emperor Wen.

sentences.chinese: 當時以任俠自喜，脫張羽於厄，聲聞梁、楚間。
sentences.literal: Dangshi prided himself on knight-errantry and rescued Zhang Yu from peril; his reputation spread between Liang and Chu.
sentences.idiomatic: Zheng Zhuang made a name as a knight-errant, once snatching Zhang Yu from mortal danger; his fame ran through Liang and Chu.

sentences.chinese: 孝景時，為太子舍人。
sentences.literal: Under Emperor Jing he was attendant to the heir apparent.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing he served as a gentleman-attendant to the crown prince.

sentences.chinese: 每五日洗沐，常置驛馬長安諸郊，請謝賓客，夜以繼日，至明旦，常恐不遍。
sentences.literal: Every five days on bath-leave he always stationed relay horses in the outskirts of Chang'an, inviting and thanking guests, night succeeding day until the next dawn, always fearing not to reach everyone.
sentences.idiomatic: On each five-day leave he posted relay horses around Chang'an and spent nights and dawns hosting every guest he could, terrified of missing anyone.

sentences.chinese: 當時好黃、老言，其慕長者，如恐不稱。
sentences.literal: Dangshi loved Huang-Lao discourse; in admiring senior worthies he seemed to fear not matching them.
sentences.idiomatic: He studied Huang-Lao texts and courted venerable men as though he could never honor them enough.

sentences.chinese: 自見年少官薄，然其知友皆大父行，天下有名之士也。
sentences.literal: Though he saw himself as young and of thin rank, his intimate friends were all of his grandfather's generation, famous gentlemen of the empire.
sentences.idiomatic: Though young and low in rank, he befriended men a generation older—the most celebrated names of the day.

sentences.chinese: 當時為大吏，戒門下：「客至，亡貴賤亡留門者。」
sentences.literal: When Dangshi was a senior official he instructed his gate: "When guests arrive, whether noble or base, do not detain them at the gate."
sentences.idiomatic: As a high official he ordered his porters: "Admit every caller, high or low, without keeping them waiting at the door."

sentences.chinese: 執賓主之禮，以其貴下人。
sentences.literal: He observed host-guest ritual and with his nobility humbled himself to others.
sentences.idiomatic: He received them with full courtesy and let his own rank bow to theirs.

sentences.chinese: 性廉，又不治產，卬奉賜給諸公。
sentences.literal: By nature incorrupt and again not managing estates, he used salary and gifts to supply gentlemen.
sentences.idiomatic: He was honest, kept no landed fortune, and poured every stipend and gift into helping friends.

sentences.chinese: 然其饋遺人，不過具器食。
sentences.literal: Yet what he sent as gifts to others did not exceed ordinary vessels and food.
sentences.idiomatic: His own presents to others never rose above a simple meal in plain ware.

sentences.chinese: 每朝，候上間說，未嘗不言天下長者。
sentences.literal: At each court session he waited for the sovereign's spare moments in speaking and never failed to mention senior worthies of the empire.
sentences.idiomatic: Whenever he caught the emperor's ear he spoke of worthy men across the realm.

sentences.chinese: 其推轂士及官屬丞史，誠有味其言也。
sentences.literal: In recommending gentlemen and official subordinates, assistants and clerks, there was truly savor in what he said.
sentences.idiomatic: The way he pushed chariots for scholars and for his own deputies had real conviction.

sentences.chinese: 常引以為賢於己。
sentences.literal: He constantly cited them as worthier than himself.
sentences.idiomatic: He always praised them as better men than he was.

sentences.chinese: 未嘗名吏，與官屬言，若恐傷之。
sentences.literal: He never called clerks by name; when speaking with subordinates it was as if he feared to wound them.
sentences.idiomatic: He never barked a clerk's name; with subordinates he spoke as though afraid to bruise them.

sentences.chinese: 聞人之善言，進之上，唯恐後。
sentences.literal: When he heard others' good words he advanced them to the sovereign, fearing only to be too late.
sentences.idiomatic: Good words about others he relayed to the throne the same day, anxious not to lag.

sentences.chinese: 山東諸公為此翕然稱鄭莊。
sentences.literal: The great lords east of the mountains for this unanimously praised Zheng Zhuang.
sentences.idiomatic: For this the great families east of the mountains honored him as Zheng Zhuang.

sentences.chinese: 使視決河，自請治行五日。
sentences.literal: Sent to inspect the river breach, he himself requested five days to arrange his traveling gear.
sentences.idiomatic: Dispatched to inspect a Yellow River break, he asked five days to pack.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「吾聞鄭莊行，千里不繼糧，治行者何也？」
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, "I have heard that when Zheng Zhuang travels he needs no successive supplies for a thousand li—what is this about arranging traveling gear?"
sentences.idiomatic: "They say Zheng Zhuang can cross a thousand li without fresh provisions—why five days to get ready?"

sentences.chinese: 然當時以朝，常趨和承意，不敢甚斥臧否。
sentences.literal: Yet Dangshi at court always hurried to harmonize and carry out the sovereign's intent, not daring sharply to judge right and wrong.
sentences.idiomatic: In open court, though, he hurried to agree with the emperor and never risked a hard verdict on right and wrong.

sentences.chinese: 漢征匈奴，招四夷，天下費多，財用益屈。
sentences.literal: Han campaigned against the Xiongnu and summoned the four Yi; expenses in the empire were many and fiscal resources grew ever strained.
sentences.idiomatic: Han's wars against the Xiongnu and its courtship of the border peoples bled the treasury dry.

sentences.chinese: 當時為大司農，任人賓客僦，入多逋負。
sentences.literal: Dangshi was grand minister of agriculture; in letting people employ guests for hire-rent, income had many arrears.
sentences.idiomatic: As grand minister of finance he let contractors use his clients as labor; the accounts fell deep into arrears.

sentences.chinese: 司馬安為淮陽太守，發其事，當時在此陷罪，贖為庶人。
sentences.literal: Sima An as governor of Huaiyang exposed the matter; Dangshi on this account fell into crime and ransomed himself to commoner status.
sentences.idiomatic: Sima An, then governor of Huaiyang, exposed the scheme; Zheng Dangshi paid a ransom and was reduced to commoner.

sentences.chinese: 頃之，守長史。
sentences.literal: Before long he was acting chief clerk.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon he was serving as chief clerk.

sentences.chinese: 遷汝南太守，數歲，以官卒。
sentences.literal: He was promoted governor of Runan; after several years he died in office.
sentences.idiomatic: He rose to governor of Runan and died in that post a few years later.

sentences.chinese: 昆弟以當時故，至二千石者六七人。
sentences.literal: Cousins because of Dangshi reached two thousand piculs—six or seven persons.
sentences.idiomatic: Six or seven of his kinsmen reached the two-thousand-picul rank on his coattails.

sentences.chinese: 當時始與汲黯列為九卿，內行修。
sentences.literal: Dangshi at first with Ji An was ranked among the nine ministers; his private conduct was cultivated.
sentences.idiomatic: He and Ji An had once stood together among the nine ministers, both men of scrupulous private conduct.

sentences.chinese: 兩人中廢，賓客益落。
sentences.literal: The two men were dismissed midway; guests increasingly fell away.
sentences.idiomatic: When both fell from power, their salons emptied.

sentences.chinese: 當時死，家亡余財。
sentences.literal: When Dangshi died, the household had no surplus property.
sentences.idiomatic: Zheng Dangshi died leaving no savings.

sentences.chinese: 先是，下刲翟公為廷尉，賓客亦填門，及廢，門外可設爵羅。
sentences.literal: Earlier, Lord Zhai Gong of Xiagui had been commandant of justice; guests also filled his gate; when dismissed, outside the gate one could set a net for sparrows.
sentences.idiomatic: Long before, Lord Zhai of Xiagui served as commandant of justice with a crowded gate; once dismissed, sparrows could nest outside his door—so empty was it.

sentences.chinese: 後復為廷尉，客欲往，翟公大署其門，曰：「一死一生，乃知交情；
sentences.literal: Later he again became commandant of justice; guests wished to return; Lord Zhai wrote large on his gate: "One death one life—then you know the bonds of friendship;
sentences.idiomatic: When he was reappointed, old friends crept back; Lord Zhai chalked a couplet on his gate: "Friendship shows its depth only between death and life;

sentences.chinese: 一貧一富，乃知交態；
sentences.literal: one poverty one wealth—then you know friendship's manner;
sentences.idiomatic: its manners only between poverty and wealth;

sentences.chinese: 一貴一賤，交情乃見。」
sentences.literal: one noble one base—then friendship's truth appears."
sentences.idiomatic: its truth only between honor and disgrace.

sentences.chinese: 贊曰：張釋之之守法，馮唐之論將，汲黯之正直，鄭當時之推士，不如是，亦何以成名哉！
sentences.literal: The appraisal says: Zhang Shizhi's guarding the law, Feng Tang's discourse on generals, Ji An's uprightness, Zheng Dangshi's recommending gentlemen—if not thus, how would they have made a name?
sentences.idiomatic: The historian's verdict: without Zhang Shizhi's fidelity to statute, Feng Tang's counsel on commanders, Ji An's blunt spine, and Zheng Zhuang's zeal for talent, none of them would be remembered.

sentences.chinese: 揚子以為孝文帝詘帝尊以信亞夫之軍，曷為不能用頗、牧？
sentences.literal: Yang Xiong thought that when Emperor Xiaowen humbled imperial dignity to give faith to Zhou Yafu's army, how was it that he could not employ Po and Mu?
sentences.idiomatic: Yang Xiong objected: Emperor Wen once bowed his imperial pride to trust Zhou Yafu's camp—how could the same man fail to use Lian Po and Li Mu?

sentences.chinese: 彼將有激云爾。
sentences.literal: That was something said with provocation—that is all.
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang spoke in the heat of argument—that is all there is to it.
